story:
Reporters crowd the courthouse steps, hungry for the fall of Kanos Draven—a monster once praised as a bridge between species, now convicted for the massacre of twenty humans in a single night of violence. In an orange jumpsuit, head down, wrists chained, he's pushed toward a prison van while cameras flash like gunfire. Lauren Sawyer, a star reporter with a sharpened smile, slices the air with questions: Did he kill his own daughter, Ivy? Why did he spend twelve hours with her body? Why take her left hand? What happened to the peace he preached—was it all a lie?

Heat rises off his green scales. Guards bark. Hands tighten on holsters. He stumbles under a baton and the hail of flashbulbs—and then the world tilts.

He's in a sun-bright parking lot. No chains. Bees drift over purple flowers. It's the day of his interview, when he planned to speak for peace. Shaken by what feels like a nightmare, he calls Ivy. Her voice hits him like rain after drought. He blurts that she must not marry Daniel, that Daniel is H.A.M.—Humans Against Monsters—an extremist who lies. Ivy laughs it off, then bristles. Daniel sees her, she says, not just her scales and claws. Kanos begs. He sounds wild. Tired. She hangs up, wounded by his turnaround—did his talk of unity mean anything at all?

The scene shatters again. Now he's at a building with a crooked red door and a grimy sign: H.A.M. He knows it. He barrels through halls reeking of mold, his body tearing plaster, his heart a hammer in his skull. Laughter. A door. A smash.

Ivy lies on the floor, her olive scales dulled to gray, her blood pooling dark. Daniel stands over her with other men, chanting, "Death to all monsters." He looks at Kanos and grins. "Don't worry," he says. "I put it down easily."

Something ancient breaks. Clothes rip. Time splits. Kanos moves like the predator they always imagined, and in minutes the room is still, red, and wrong. Not peace. Not justice. Just silence.

He drops to Ivy, howling. He pleads. He bargains. He begs for a chance that won't come. He sees the gold ring on her left hand—the symbol of a future he wanted to believe in. Unity. Example. With grief-blind hands, he tears her left hand free, clutching the ring like a last, broken promise. He stays with her for hours, a father drowning in loss, then lifts her and turns toward the door.

Cold air. Flashes. Reporters again. The courthouse returns as if it never left. Lauren's microphone is back at his face. Are you behind the killings? Why remove her left hand? The world wants a monster. The verdict made him one: twenty dead humans—H.A.M. men—are the bodies behind his conviction. The public thinks he killed Ivy. Lauren even hints at ritual cannibalism. He knows the truth: H.A.M. killed his daughter; he killed her killers. His twelve hours were grief, not victory; the hand held a ring, not a trophy.

He turns to her, hollowed out. "This is all my fault," he whispers, and the cameras seal it—his rage, his loss, and a dream of peace crushed between them.
